input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT employs SysML v1.7 for architectural modeling, MontiArc automaton for behavior definition, SysML IBDs for interface specification, simulation via MathWorks Simulink, and code generation in C++ and Java. The MBSE process is supported by methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to manage abstraction levels and stakeholder integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellation orchestrates the system’s architectural description across multiple modeling languages and simulation tools within a central MBSE framework.  Component and connector aspects are encoded using architecture description languages such as ACME or AADL, while SysML Interaction Block Diagrams (IBDs) capture functional and logical interactions.  Behavioral detail is embodied either in MontiArc Automata or in SysML v1.7 state machines, and simulation models are integrated via MathWorks Simulink or equivalent tools, allowing the tight coupling of discrete‑time heterogeneous models.  This multi‑notation, component‑centric orchestration provides coordinated resource management by linking the static structural model with its dynamic simulation counterpart, thereby sustaining a coherent end‑to‑end Digital Twin representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation is structured using Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) artefacts that span multiple abstraction levels—requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers—defined with SysML IBDs for component‑and‑connector structure. Functional and logical architectures are captured with architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, or MontiArc. Behavioral models are represented either through MontiArc Automaton blocks or SysML v1.7 state machines. These models are executed or simulated using MathWorks Simulink along with additional simulation tools or code compiled in languages such as C++ or Java. Data sources for the Twin include contextual traces captured by digital shadow abstractions (set of contextual data traces and their aggregation) and processed through active DT components. The data‑management pipeline relies on model relationships enforced by the chosen ADLs, with data associations mapped by the modeling notation. Thus the computational models comprise geometric/structural representations (SysML component diagrams), behavioral automata (MontiArc), and analytical simulations (Simulink) that are integrated to provide real‑time or offline Digital Twin functionality.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle is delineated by successive abstraction levels: 1) Requirements capturing the functional intent and performance criteria; 2) Functional architecture that defines capabilities and interfaces; 3) Logical architecture that specifies component behaviours and interaction patterns; 4) Product or technical layer that models concrete implementation details and material properties. Transitions between stages are managed via model transformations and conformance checks in SysML specification diagrams (e.g., internal block diagrams, state machine diagrams). Lifecycle governance is achieved through Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) practices that enforce traceability and versioning across these layers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows MBSE workflows employing SysML as the primary system modelling language, complemented by architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML internal block diagrams. Design methods such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT guide cost, reliability and availability analysis and system integration. Behaviour is described through state‑machine diagrams and MontiArc automaton, and simulation is performed with MathWorks Simulink or other numerical tools. Software components that interpret incoming telemetry are implemented in general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java, and version management is defined by standard MBSE version control (e.g., Git). The evolution of a Digital Twin is managed through iterative refinement of these models, with conformance checks across the abstraction layers and a continuous integration pipeline that re‑runs simulation tests upon model changes.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
